# Stock Options Tax Guide 2026: ISO vs NSO and AMT

## Summary
2026 tax treatment comparison for ISO (Incentive Stock Options) and NSO (Non-Qualified Stock Options). NSOs are taxed as ordinary income at exercise (up to 37% federal + state); ISOs trigger no regular income tax at exercise but create an AMT preference item. ISOs qualify for long-term capital gains treatment if held 1 year after exercise AND 2 years after grant date. ISO eligibility is restricted to employees of C-corporations only, with a $100,000 annual grant limit per employee. California state tax treats ISO exercises as ordinary income regardless of federal ISO status. The 2026 AMT exemption for married filing jointly is $153,950. Early exercise with an 83(b) election can lock in value and minimize ordinary income tax exposure.

## Key quotations / statistics
- "NSOs: Spread taxed as ordinary income at exercise at up to 37% federal + state rates"
- "ISOs: No regular income tax at exercise; Spread becomes an AMT preference item—may trigger AMT at 26-28%"
- ISOs "qualify for long-term capital gains (0%, 15%, or 20%) if held 1 year after exercise AND 2 years after grant date"
- "Annual grant limit [for ISOs]: $100,000 FMV per employee"
- "Limited to employees only; C-corporations only" for ISOs
- "California treats ISO exercises as ordinary income for state tax purposes regardless of federal ISO status, taxing the spread at exercise at up to 13.3%"
- "2026 AMT exemption for married filing jointly is $153,950"
- "Filing an 83(b) election within 30 days of exercising restricted/unvested shares can lock in value"

## Annotations for stinger-forge
- This is the supporting source for the ISO/NSO overview in `guides/05-option-pool-management.md`. The stinger's Command Brief scopes this as an "overview" not a deep tax guide.
- The $100,000 ISO grant limit per employee is a common surprise for founders issuing large grants - include this in the guide with a practical note about what happens when the limit is exceeded (excess converts to NSO treatment).
- The California ISO state-tax treatment (ordinary income despite federal ISO status) is a critical jurisdiction-specific warning for founders with California-based employees - flag with a state-specific callout.
- The 83(b) election cross-reference connects back to the peer stinger (incorporation-startup-stack-stinger) which covers the 83(b) in detail. Flag this in the guide with "see also: incorporation-startup-stack-stinger".
- The AMT exemption figure ($153,950 MFJ in 2026) is useful context for employees considering large ISO exercises.
